data.title: Ford's Q3 Earnings: +38% PHEV Sales Increase And Declining EBIT
data.description:
Among the Detroit Big Three, only GM seems to be sailing through a difficult market with some degree of success. On the other hand, Stellantis (STLA) and Ford (NYSE:F) (NEOE:F:CA) have their headaches to face. After all, S&P Global has recently warned about the tough road ahead of the auto industry due to credit deterioration and weaker-than-expected sales.

As we approach Ford's Q3 earnings report, some pieces of the puzzle we need to solve when shaping a preview is already available. In particular, in addition to Ford's guidance at the end of Q2, we already know Ford's Q3 sales.

At the end of Q2, Ford updated its FCF guidance for FY2024, increasing it by $1B to $8.5B and targeting a return between 40% and 50% of its FCF to shareholders. Thanks to a strong balance sheet with $27B in cash and $45 in available liquidity, Ford's returns seem sustainable. Net of debt, Ford holds $6.2B, which is decent.

However, at the end of Q2, Ford's EBIT was down by 27% YoY to $2.8B, with an EBIT margin of 5.8%. In particular, the company's electric vehicle business is still unprofitable and reported a negative $1.1B in EBIT in just one quarter. For the first half, EBIT was negative by $2.5B, which is quite a hit. Ford, on the other hand, has a highly profitable business in the pro segment, where its EBIT margins hover around 16%. In other words, Pro is financing EVs. This was the focus of a topic Ford Pro CFO Navin Kumar was asked by Adam Jones at Morgan Stanley's 12th Annual Laguna Conference:

Year-to-date, Ford Pro's first-half EBIT is $5.6 billion. Ford's total company EBIT is $5.5 billion. Ford Pro, again, I'm brilliant at math, is over 100% of total company EBIT. A common criticism from investors, including many in this room, is that Ford Pro generates all the damn cash, all the cash of the company and that, that money is going to pay for loss-making EVs and warranty costs. Is that fair?

The question was left unanswered.

Ford reported a 4% retail sales increase in the third quarter, which contrasts with flat sales in the U.S. market. Hybrid vehicles are being favored, with a 38% YoY increase to 48,101 units, while EVs increased only by 12% YoY. However, Ford sold 67,689 EVs through September and claims to be second only to Tesla in the U.S.

Ford's leadership in trucks, with its F-series sales outselling the Chevrolet Silverado by 138,000 units and the Ram by a whopping 282,000 trucks. Moreover, F-150 hybrid sales were up 64% YoY to 20,129 units, topping even the Ford Maverick, whose sales rose 22% to 16,561.

ICE vehicles, on the other hand, saw their sales decrease by over 12,000 units to 432,429 in the quarter, a 2.8% YoY decline. YtD, ICE sales are down 1.8%, but total vehicle sales are up 2.7% to 1,548,172 units. In a softening market, this is a very good result. Ford has three main types of vehicles: SUVs, trucks, and cars. Their sales are all up YtD, but in Q3, SUVs reported a 5.6% decline and car sales were down 8.2%. However, cars are a minor segment, with only 9,000 units sold in the quarter. It is the segment that reports the Mustang sales. Trucks take the lion's shares with 292,010 vehicles of the 504,039 Ford sold in the third quarter (58% of the mix). Lincoln brand seems recovering and reported a 26% YoY increase in sales, thanks to the Nautilus and the Aviator.

Compared to Ford's sales report in Q3 2023, inventory is up from 424,400 units to 587,700. This confirms the trend we had read in Ford's quarterly reports, with the TTM inventory up to $17.18B vs. $15.65B at the end of FY2023. What we see from the Q3 sales report is a 38.5% YoY increase in units, and I am expecting the earnings call to address this trend with an explanation of what the company plans on doing to keep it under control. While Ford Pro has the margins to discount a bit, the rest of Ford can't afford more discounts if it wants to run at a profit.

While we read the October sales report, we should not, in any case, overlook an issue that eroded Ford's profitability in Q2 and might probably have an impact on Q3 as well: increased warranty costs associated with its EV division. Just to give an idea, the word "warranty" occurred 26 times in the last earnings call, and Ford's President and CEO Jim Farley could only say the company is trying to improve its quality but still anticipates higher warranty costs down the road for this fiscal year. In an environment where a pricing war seems to be ramping up rapidly with the aggressive moves of Chinese OEMs, it is not a great thing to have this kind of cost.

Now, Ford's balance sheet is in good shape, with $18.7B in industrial debt and over $26B in cash and available liquidity. But if its profits keep being eroded, and we see its EBITDA plunge below $6B we would have its industrial debt/EBITDA ratio spike up above 3x, which is not a good sign. In this case, we might be seeing Ford reconsider once again a dividend cut. After all, Ford is indeed guiding for FCF of $8.5B, but it also spends around $8B in R&D.

Ford Q3 Earnings Preview

So, the picture is mixed. Ford sales seem to be holding up quite well, but we have its profitability under scrutiny. After all, for the last 3 months, we have 18 analysts releasing an EPS down revision while only one increased its estimates. At the same time, Ford's revenue has 8 up revisions and 4 down revisions. This means analysts are mainly expecting a deteriorating profitability.

In Q3, Ford should report around $42B in revenue, which would be close to a 2% YoY growth. I believe we might actually see Ford top this threshold, given the strong performance of Ford Pro. However, I think the current EPS estimate of $0.48 for Q3 might be a bit too optimistic since it represents a 23.5% YoY growth.

After all, Ford's net income margin is around 2-3%. Therefore, we could expect around $1B in net income. Considering Ford has 4B shares outstanding, we would have Ford's Q3 EPS around $0.26, which would mean flat growth YoY. Now, it is true that Ford is guiding for $2B in manufacturing savings this year, thanks to lower inflation and cheaper raw material prices. So, it would not be absurd to assume Ford could report a 4% net income margin, which would make Ford report $1.68B in net income. If we consider that there might be a probable headwind coming from warranty costs, let's place our estimate at $1.5B in Q3 net income. In this case, our EPS estimate jumps up to $0.38. I think this is a more reasonable forecast than the current consensus. What does this mean? That the FY EPS estimate might fall short of the $1.87 analysts are expecting, which puts Ford at a 6 fwd PE. The fwd PE might actually be closer to 6.5. Now, although this may seem dirt cheap, it is not. Remember, we are talking about an automaker. A cheap PE in this industry is a 2 or a 3. Above a 6 we are somewhat talking about a premium. Of course, Ford Pro is a strong business. But, as we have seen, it is being diluted by Ford's other two business lines.

Not by chance, Ford is one of the few automakers not earning an A+ as its Quant Factor Valuation Grade. GM, which is currently performing better, trades at a fwd PE just above a 5. The same is true with the more troubled Stellantis. Volkswagen trades at a fwd PE of 3. This means Ford is twice as expensive as Volkswagen right now. But I find this valuation hard to justify.

As a result, although I really like the Ford Pro business, I don't see great value right now in this stock. These earnings, in addition, may see the stock go through some volatility since investors might get nervous if Ford's warranty issues keep being reported. Given the current environment and the available data, I would stay neutral. Therefore, I rate the stock as a hold heading into earnings.

data.title: Madison Diversified Income Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Recap – Third Quarter 2024

After a rough start, U.S. stocks bounced back to generate another impressive gain, with the S&P 500 Index up 5.9% for the third quarter of 2024, pushing the year-to-date total return to a staggering 22.1%, not far off 2023’s outsized 26.3% return, with three months left to go in the year.

If you hadn’t heard the term “yen carry trade” before, it was likely burned into your lexicon by early August. The rapid ascent of the formerly shunned Japanese currency was behind a nasty bout of volatility that took U.S. stocks down nearly 10%, as investors were forced to quickly unwind levered investments and prevent further losses as the yen moved against them. Behind the yen’s rise was a stark contrast in the outlook for monetary policy between the U.S. and Japan, which came to a head in the final week of July when Federal Reserve Chair Powell leaned dovish while the Bank of Japan hawkishly raised interest rates, then a sour U.S. employment report added fuel to the rate cut fire. However, the Bank of Japan yielded via softer comments around future policy and the markets went back to rallying on the belief of much easier monetary policy (lower interest rates) moving forward in the U.S.

The shifting market sentiment towards the anticipation of even lower rates propelled cyclically-based and smaller cap stocks to big gains, especially early in the quarter.

Technology stocks were notable underperformers and the mixed performance of the so- called Magnificent 7, along with other mega cap stocks led to a broader and seemingly healthier market advance. Overall, the small cap Russell 2000 Index jumped 9.3% versus the S&P 500’s 5.9% return, and the more economically sensitive Russell 1000 Value Index gained 9.4%, well above the 3.2% return for the technology-heavy Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Big gains were not just for stocks. Bond returns were boosted by a steep drop in interest rates across the yield curve from the increased expectations of deeper Fed rate cuts.

Entering the quarter, investors expected the Fed Funds rate to fall just short of 0.5% by year end, and largely expected the Fed to cut by the standard 0.25% for their highly anticipated first rate cut of the cycle on September 18th. However, belief in something bigger built after Chair Powell’s comment that “the time has come for policy to adjust”

during his remarks at Jackson Hole in August, as he detailed his growing confidence that inflation is under control and that the Fed is now more concerned about the cooling labor market. As the September Fed meeting approached, market odds firmed around a larger 0.5% cut, and on that fateful day,

the Fed delivered with a jumbo half-point cut to kick-off the cutting cycle and guided for another 0.5% of cuts to come by January. However, market optimism ran higher, exiting the quarter with an implied rate 0.75% below today’s level, with dreams of a 3% Fed Funds rate by the end of 2025. The falling interest rate hoopla drove the broad Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index to a near S&P 500 matching 5.2% return for the quarter, bringing the nine-month total up to 4.5%.

Outside of the U.S., China pushed the Bank of Japan out of the spotlight by launching a massive stimulus plan during the final week of the quarter. Investors welcomed Beijing’s willingness to turn the monetary spigot wide open by bidding Chinese equity indexes up over 20% in seven days. The upward explosion of Chinese stocks helped boost international stocks and the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index to an 8.1% return, bettering the U.S. market for the quarter.

Performance

The broadening of the market beyond the small set of very large U.S. companies benefited the fund for the quarter. Dividend stocks had a particularly strong showing as evidenced by the Dow Jones Select Dividend Index return of 13.1%. Over 40% of the fund’s equity allocation is comprised of high-quality dividend stocks, making dividend payers the overwhelming top contributor to performance. Our positions in mortgage-backed securities and high-grade corporate bond ETFs also contributed positively to performance. Detracting from relative performance was our covered call ETF and modest allocation to an MLP ETF.

Outlook And Positioning

Our primary rationale for remaining cautious in our asset allocation positioning hinges on the dramatic difference in market expectations and pricing between the bond and commodities markets compared to the stock market. Starting with the bond market, as of the end of the third quarter, Fed Funds futures are pricing in nearly 2% of additional rate cuts over the next 12 months, which would bring the Fed Funds rate to around 3%. Cutting rates at that speed and magnitude has historically only occurred in a recessionary environment. The price of oil declined by 20% during the quarter, surveys of manufacturing activity remain contractionary, the index of leading economic indicators has not recorded a positive reading since February 2022, a month before the Fed began to tighten monetary policy. These are not typical signposts of a healthy economy. On the other hand, the S&P 500 charges on to repeated all-time highs, with an atypically-high valuation level and an expectation for nearly 15% earnings growth for 2025. Whatever a “soft landing” might be, we don’t believe this is it. If the bond market is correct, we are headed for an economic reckoning in the near future and stocks are due for a rude awakening. If the stock market is correct, the economic expansion is set to continue without needing a landing. In that case, it’s highly unlikely that we’ll see 2% of rate cuts over the next year and longer-term rates have fallen too far.

What’s clear is that one market has it wrong. The resolution of this condition will likely be quite volatile. We believe the bond market appears closer to fair value than stocks. As such, we remain moderately underweight equities given the highly uncertain and contradictory environment.

Disclosures

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

The S&P 500® Index is a large-cap market index which measures the performance of a representative sample of 500 leading companies in leading industries in the U.S.

Russell Investment Group is the source and owner of the trademarks, service marks and copyrights related to the Russell Indexes. Russell® is a trademark of Russell Investment Group.

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 11% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000® Index.

The Russell Midcap® Index measures the performance of the 800 smallest companies in the Russell 1000® Index, which represent approximately 35% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 1000® Index.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index is designed to track those securities within the broader Russell 1000 Index that FTSE Russell has determined exhibit growth characteristics.

The Russell 1000® Value Index is designed to track those securities within the broader Russell 1000 Index that FTSE Russell has determined exhibit value characteristics.

The MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index captures large and mid cap representation across 22 of 23 Developed Markets countries (excl. the US) and 23 Emerging Markets countries. With 1,843 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global equity opportunity set outside the US.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index of U.S. fixed income securities. The U.S. Aggregate Index covers the USD- denominated, investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable bond market of SEC-registered securities. The index includes bonds from the Treasury, Government-Related, Corporate, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM passthroughs), ABS, and CMBS sectors.

The Dow Jones U.S. Select Dividend Index aims to measure the performance of high dividend-paying U.S. stocks, excluding REITs, meeting specific criteria for dividends, earnings, size and liquidity. It uses a yield-driven weighting method.

Yield Curve is a line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. There are three main types of yield curve shapes: normal (upward sloping curve), inverted (downward sloping curve) and flat.

The Magnificent Seven stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla.

In addition to the ongoing market risk applicable to portfolio securities, bonds are subject to interest rate risk, credit risk and inflation risk. When interest rates rise, bond prices fall; generally, the longer a bond’s maturity, the more sensitive it is to this risk. Credit risk is the possibility that the issuer of a security will be unable to make interest payments and repay the principal on its debt. Bonds may also be subject to call risk, which allows the issuer to retain the right to redeem the debt, fully or partially, before the scheduled maturity date.

Proceeds from sales prior to maturity may be more or less than originally invested due to changes in market conditions or changes in the credit quality of the issuer.

The home office for each firm listed above is 550 Science Drive, Madison, WI 53711. Madison’s toll-free number is 800-767-0300.

data.title: Maui Land & Pineapple Outlook Remains Strong
data.description:
I have written an article on the Maui Land & Pineapple Company, Inc. previously. Anyone interested in reading that article can find it here.

I am placing a BUY rating on the Maui Land & Pineapple Company, Inc. (NYSE:MLP) because I believe that the land that the company owns could be worth up to 580.88% more than the company's current share price would suggest. While there are unfortunately major impediments to being able to realize the full value of all of the land the company owns, just the land that MLP is currently in the process of having developed could be worth up to 197.75% of the company's current share price of $23.53.

About MLP

The Maui Land & Pineapple Company, Inc. was incorporated in 1909 in Lahaina, Hawaii. The company currently owns approximately 22,300 acres in Maui which it currently runs leasing, a resort, and real estate operations from. The company used to run pineapple plantations from some of its properties but shut down operations in 2009 after years of unprofitable harvests. Since then the company's primary source of income has been running its Kapalua resort which includes collecting membership fees and leasing spaces for businesses.

MLP's Income Statement

MLP's existing business operations don't make a lot of money and the business has posted a loss in operating income every year since 2018 except for 2022. While the company had clearly seen some operational difficulties, in 2020 the COVID-19 pandemic really did it in for the Hawaiian tourism industry. The state's proximity to Asia and Australia, which had strict travel rules and regulations during the pandemic helped to significantly curtail Maui's tourism industry. This led to MLP's fiscal 2020 and 2021 showing continued net losses before finally posting $1.8 million in net income in 2022. Unfortunately this wouldn't last for long.

As MLP was finally recovering from multiple years of losing money another tragedy fell into MLP's lap. In 2023 Maui experienced a series of wildfires that destroyed much of West Maui. While MLP was lucky in the sense that none of their land was destroyed in these fires, the fires burned down parts of Lahaina directly south of the company's Kapalua resort. This tragedy killed and displaced a lot of people and again disrupted the island's economy, especially its tourism revenue. This predictably led the company towards another year of net losses. MLP's net income figures for their fiscal 2023 were also negatively affected by the company hiring colliers to assess the business's land value. While the colliers are in the process of assessing this property MLP has refrained from placing any of their property on sale.

It's been a little over a year since the Maui wildfires and it looks like MLP's business operations may be starting to trend in the right direction. According to the company's last earnings call MLP saw a 24% increase in commercial real estate occupancy over the past year which has helped them to increase their operating revenue by 7.5%. With what is hopefully the last bit of tragedy and sorrow for the people of Maui and the island's economy behind them, MLP can focus on continuing to move the company's operations back towards being profitable again.

While MLP's resort and property leasing operations are not the main focus of my buy rating, if MLP does make a turn around and begins to show operations and net income profits, the share price could spike on this news alone. The most important thing that will happen once MLP returns to operational profitability is that the company can then fund its future land developments with cash from normal operations versus taking on debt, issuing more stock, or selling off land.

MLP's Properties

MLP owns approximately 22,300 acres of land. 20,400 acres of this land is located in West Maui, 9,000 acres of which is made up of the Pu’u Kukui Watershed Preserve. 1,000 acres of this land in West Maui is made up of the Kapalua Resort. This resort is a master-planned, destination resort, and residential community. This resort is also home to the Ritz Carlton, Maui and features a number of world class golf courses, tennis courts, with access to things like ziplining, hiking, mountain biking, world class food, beer, wine, liquor, full spas, some of the most beautiful beaches in the world, non-stop group activities, and various forms of professional world class entertainment.

The remaining chunks of land that MLP owns in West Maui are located south of the Kapalua resort and consist largely of agricultural land that stretches between the Kapalua resort and the town of Lahaina. The chunks of property to the north of the Kapalua resort look to be compromised of unimproved land on mountainous terrain that butts up to a forest reserve. MLP has another approximately 1,400 acres of property located in Upcountry Maui in an area known to the locals as Hali'imaile. This property is primarily made up of leased agricultural fields, commercial, and light industrial properties.

Average Property Values

880 Acres in Kapalua

Right now MLP has entitlement rights on 880 acres of land to build 1,300 housing units in Kapalua. The entitlements to build housing on this land also includes entitlements to build commercial properties and amenities for these communities. This property sits right next to the Kapalua Central Resort and will sell for a much higher price than MLP's other acreage, especially considering all of the amenities that immediate area has to offer. I looked up the cost of land in Kapalua and averaged out the price per acre for all nine properties that were up for sale at the time. While the price of each lot depended on multiple factors including proximity to and the view of the ocean, proximity to the resort, and an assortment of other variables, the average cost per acre in Kapalua ended up being $1.73 million per acre.

Hali`imaile Town

MLP also has 1,400 acres located in an area known as Hali`imaile. This property is located in the Upcountry Maui region of the island and isn't worth as much as MLP's Kapalua acreage is. When I looked at 39 separate land parcels in the area they averaged out at a much smaller $664,642 an acre. The company plans to have a master-planned community built on 290 acres of this land starting in 2029 with an additional 10 acres to support community infrastructure. As of now MLP has not made any direct comment on how it intends to utilize the remaining 1,200 acres of land in Hali`imaile.

The Land Between Kapalua and Lahaina

MLP has 5,920 acres of land between Kapalua and Lahaina that it currently leases out to farmers for agricultural purposes. This property runs right along highway 30. Many of these lots of agricultural land are located directly across from other housing and looks like the perfect location for future development. Without the land being entitled and without MLP making a comment on the direction they'd like to go with this property it is hard to place a price on it. That being said, it is hard to imagine land this pristine located directly between the two of the most sought after places in Maui selling for less than $500,000 an acre.

Properties the Company Might Not Sell

While MLP owns 22,300 acres of land in Hawaii. Management has stated that it currently only has plans to build on 1,170 acres of it. That leaves a remaining 21,130 acres at the company's disposal. Of this acreage 9,000 of these acres belong to the Pu‘u Kukui Watershed preserve. It's with a great deal of certainty that I say this land will probably never have housing built on it. It's a nature preserve that has an enormous amount of ecological importance to the island.

MLP also owns another 5,370 acres just north of the Kapalua resort. I am excluding all of this property from my valuation because this land appears to be full of mountainous terrain and does not appear to have ever had fields plowed into it the way their agricultural acreage south of the Kapalua resort does. This northern acreage also bumps up to the West Maui Forest Reserve. Land this close to important natural resources would likely make things like entitlement rights difficult to procure.

I wouldn't be surprised if these properties were to be gifted back to Hawaii in exchange for entitlement rights for the company to build on its other properties south of the Kapalua resort. These plots of land are much better suited to have housing built on them. Most of this property has been used for agricultural purposes and therefore is already cleared and improved land. These land plots are also much closer to other housing on Maui which puts them much nearer in proximity to existing infrastructure on the island.

The Value of MLP's Land Versus MLP's Share Price

The Value of MLP's Current Projects

Once we have some estimates on land values for the areas around which MLP owns its properties, we can start to get a sense of how much each piece of property MLP owns is worth. If MLP's 880 acres of land developments at Kapalua get sold for $1.73 million an acre, the company will earn $1.52 billion from the sale. If you applied a 21.0% rate for the tax MLP would likely pay on the sale of that land you end up with $1.20 billion. When dividing that figure by MLP's 19.65 million shares outstanding you come to a value of $61.29 per share. That's a 140.07% upside from the stocks current share price from just one company excursion into developing its land.

If through the same process we take the 290 acres at Hali`imaile and multiply them by the average price per acre in Upcountry Maui we reach a value of $192.75 million. After we subtract 21.0% of that value for taxes we can take the $152.27 million figure we get and divide that by the company's outstanding shares. We end up with $7.75 per share's worth of value from the company's Hali`imaile properties set to have a new community built on them. From these property values alone I am giving MLP a buy rating.

The Value of MLP's Remaining Land With Saleable Prospects

The Value of MLP's Property South of the Kapalua Resort.

Without MLP's other 5,920 acres of land between Kapalua and Lahaina currently being entitled for housing and without MLP making any comments on what direction they'd like to go with this property, it's hard to place an accurate price on it. Even if we assume that MLP was only able to get half of this land entitled and that once entitled this property was to only have a value of $500,000 an acre, we still get a sale price of $1.48 billion.

Even after subtracting 21.0% of this value to account for taxes, if MLP's 2,960 acres south of their Kapalua resort sold at $500,000 an acre it would still net the company $1.17 billion. When dividing this by the company's 19.65 million shares outstanding we get a 152.87% upside in the value of this property versus MLP's share price. Even when subtracting MLP's small $0.39 per share's worth of liabilities from this value we are still left with at $59.11 per share's worth of value, giving us a 151.21% upside.

The Value of the Rest of MLP's Hali`imaile Property

MLP's other roughly 1,200 acres of land in Hali`imaile is currently zoned mostly for agricultural use. If the company can get these properties entitled and sold off for a normal Upcountry Maui average of $664,642 an acre MLP could bring in another $797.57 million. After a 21.0% tax rate is subtracted from this value MLP would still come out with another $630.08 million from this transaction. After the company's liabilities are subtracted from this value we end up with $31.67 per share's worth of value, a 34.59% upside from the company's current share price.

MLP's Target Share Price

Based Off of Expected Land Sales

If we take the value per share of MLP's 880 acres at Kapalua and the company's 290 acres at Hali`imaile and add them together, then subtract the business's total liabilities from that value, we get a value target of $68.65 per share. That's a 191.75% upside compared to the company's current share price of $23.53. This $68.65 is my current share price target for the Maui Land & Pineapple company.

MLP's other properties are still surrounded by large levels of uncertainty. Besides hiring colliers the company has not made any official announcements with what it plans to do with this land. Actively pursuing entitlements on the remaining properties that appear to be suitable for future housing could come with its own set of head aches. It can be difficult to obtain building permit in Hawaii. In fact, in 2022 Hawaii topped the nation for building permit delays. I expect that these delays will stick around well into the future and make MLP's prospects of being able to realize the full value of its properties much less likely.

MLP's current master-planned community projects on the other hand, may experience some sort of sped-up process. This is due in large part to the Maui wildfires that ravaged the island in 2023. A little more than a year after the fires scorched Lahaina many of the buildings are still destroyed and many of its residents are still waiting to see if their homes are going to be rebuilt. For a state that has long suffered from a severe housing shortage, these wildfires exacerbated the problem leaving residents angry at the bottle neck effect of Maui's process to acquire building permits. With this much pressure being placed on local politicians to speed up the rebuilding process I have some hope that these 1,170 acres of property that MLP wants to build housing on will be shoed along quite nicely.

Based Off of Theoretically Possible Land Sales

If we take the $69.04 per share's worth of value of MLP's 880 acres at Kapalua and their 290 acres at Hali`imaile and add in the rest of the company's properties that look to have future prospects of being sold you can see the extreme potential for long term value that's hidden in this stock. The remaining $32.06 per share's worth of property at Hali`imaile and the $59.50 per share's worth of value the company has locked away in just half of its land holdings south of Kapalua add a combined $91.56 per share's worth of value potential to MLP's properties. That ends up being $160.60 worth of value per share from all of MLP's properties it has a realistic probability of developing in the future. That figure ends up at $160.21 per share in net value after factoring in the company's liabilities, a 580.88% increase from the current share price of $23.53.

Management

There are other public companies in Hawaii that also appear to be significantly undervalued in relationship to their property holdings. While company's like Kaanapali Land, LLC (OTCPK:KANP) may end up profiting handsomely off of the property they own, MLP has some unique advantages. MLP is already moving forward with plans to build up some of its properties. This is a far bigger deal in Hawaii than it is in many other states as Hawaii has built up a notorious reputation for being extremely difficult to build on due to those abundant regulatory hurdles. While the Maui wildfires may put some pressure on the local government to speed up their regulatory processes I wouldn't bet on it.

This is precisely why I believe that MLP is much better positioned to benefit from their property holdings on Hawaii than other companies are. MLP has land it can gift back to the government in exchange for entitlements to its other properties that have significantly higher use values. While having a bunch of somewhat disposable property lying around in your asset portfolio can be helpful, MLP's true advantage is the company's management team. MLP has been diligently funneling in a talented array of people that appear to be tailor made to build houses on Maui.

I wrote pretty extensively about MLP's management team in my last MLP article that was published in March of 2024. My thesis and comments on Steve Case, John Sabin, Glyn Aeppel, and Scot Sellers are just as relevant today as they were in March, thus my writing on them will remain unchanged. The company did, however, add two other members to their roster back in January that I didn't get a chance to adequately cover in my last article. The appointment of these two new independent directors should prove to have huge benefits to shareholders.

Ken Ota

Ota founded the Pacific Pipe Company as President and CEO. In 2021 Core and Main, Inc. acquired the Pacific Pipe Company where Ota currently serves as the Hawaii area director. Core and Main, Inc. is a distributor of water, wastewater, storm drainage and fire protection products. This plays into MLP's business strategy as the company owns several potable water wells, non-potable irrigation water ditches, reservoirs, and transmission systems that provide sales of potable and non-potable water in West and Upcountry Maui.

Ota is rather uniquely qualified to be able to serve MLP's very specific needs in this regard as well as possibly provide further improvements to this water infrastructure as the communities around these areas expand. Another major win for MLP is the fact that Ota is currently on the State of Hawaii Licensing Board of Engineers, Architects, Surveyors, and Landscape Architects. This is possibly the most important piece of Ota's qualifications to work with MLP. Without approval from these boards MLP can't build anything. Having a company board member with a massive amount of knowledge about all of the details involved with getting approvals to build in Maui will truly be invaluable. His addition to MLP as an Independent Director is exactly what the company needs to move forward with its building plans.

Catherine Ngo

Ngo was an Executive Vice Chair person and is now serving as Chair of the Central Pacific Financial Corp (NYSE:CPF), a Hawaiian bank based in Honolulu. For six years prior to that Ngo served as the President and CEO of Central Pacific Bank. A University of Virginia School of Law graduate Ngo spent seven years practicing private law before becoming the Executive Vice President of Silicon Valley Bank until 2005 when she helped found Startup Capital Ventures as a general partner. She is an active board and committee member of the Queen’s Health Systems, Hawaii Community Foundation, and Catholic Charities of Hawaii. Ngo also serves as a board member for Hawaii Gas, chairs the Federal Reserve Bank Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council for the 12th District, and is a member of the National FRB CDIAC in Washington, D.C.

Ngo's extensive roles held within the State of Hawaii help give MLP an edge when it comes to being able to complete their upcoming building projects. Her ties to the legal, banking, and capital venture worlds should give her the experience she needs to help steer the company through the various legal, regulatory, and finance hurdles that companies often face when undertaking a building project of this scale. The relationships she has built given the fact that much of her professional career has taken place in Hawaii is an enormous asset. While you can rarely put precise values on longstanding business relationships, building them throughout your career can undoubtedly foster business environments that can prove extremely beneficial.

(Below are the members of MLP that I wrote about in my last article.)

Steve Case

Steve Case is an MLP board member and is MLP's largest shareholder. Case owns 62% of the company, originally buying a 41% stake in the company back in 1999 before increasing his stake in 2010. Case was a co-founder of AOL and was still a board member when he originally purchased a stake in MLP. AOL had been undergoing some major difficulties and Case's purchase was at the time very poorly received.

One of the reasons why I think the Maui Land and Pineapple Company has such great potential is because Case is actually from Hawaii and bought this land not just because he thought he could make money out of this investment one day but because he really does seem to have a keen interest in preserving some of the paradise he grew up in. Case's lifelong understanding of Hawaii should play a major role in the success of MLP's land development endeavors. Case's father Daniel Case was a well-known lawyer in Hawaii and his mother Carol Case was a school teacher. Case's cousin is currently a congressman in the House of Representatives servings as a Democrat, so needless to say, I think Case has his roots set in Hawaii.

The other equally important reason why I think that MLP has so much potential is Case's patience. The building arm of his Revolution LLC company Revolution Places has developed other plots of land into extravagant resorts that turned into handsome profits for those involved. In 2022 Revolution Places finally broke ground on a large Waldorf Astoria hotel in Costa Rica that it intends to open in 2025. Revolution Places had acquired the property in 2007. When asked by Forbes contributor Carrie Coolidge what took him so long to build Case replied “It was more important to do it right. So we spent more than a decade finding the right partners who wanted to honor this special place.”

That statement leads me to my next set of management team introductions. Since 2022 MLP has strategically brought on 3 new members to its board of directors, and a new CEO. The members of this team are frankly almost too talented for a small company like MLP with a $480 million market cap and it is my belief that they wouldn't have jumped on board with this company unless they were absolutely convinced that MLP's potential for future value generation far exceeds what is reflected on the company's financial statements today.

John Sabin

Long time CFO for two of Steve Case's companies, Revolution LLC and Case Foundation, John Sabin is always close by Case's property development endeavors. Sabin was appointed to MLP's Board of Directors in July of 2022. He has a long history and a lot of experience with various real estate and hospitality organizations under an assortment of legal and executive duties.

Glyn Aeppel

Aeppel was also appointed to MLP's Board of Directors in July of 2022. Aeppel has an extremely impressive record as Founder and CEO of "Glencove Capital the only woman-owned and operated hospitality investment firm in the United States." Le Meridien, Fairmont, Loews, and Marriott are just a few of the household names she has worked with. Aeppel has over 35 years of experience developing hotel brands and "has sourced, closed, and financed real estate investments of well over $3 billion" in both the United States and Europe. She also serves on the board of Simon Property Group.

Scot Sellers

Sellers is probably the perfect man for MLP. He sits on the boards of two of the most prominent companies in the United States that build Master Planned Communities which is exactly what MLP plans to build in Kapalua and Hali`imaile. Sellers used to be the CEO of Archstone, one of the largest multi-family housing companies in the world. He was CEO for almost 20 years where he grew it from "several hundred million dollars in assets to a total capitalization exceeding $22 billion." Archstone (by then called Archstone-Smith) was sold to Tishman Speyer and Lehman Brothers Holdings Inc. in a deal that was estimated to be worth $13.5 billion. Scot joined the MLP Board of Directors in March of 2023.

Race Randle

Race Randle is a native Hawaiian that joined MLP in March of 2023 as CEO. His accomplishments include a redesign on Ward Village in Honolulu that involved building 4,300 residential units on 60 acres of land. This redesign was meant to be an eco-friendly design that promotes healthy lifestyles by creating a community where vehicles are largely unnecessary, while simultaneously merging island life with the needs that come from an urban environment. Ward Village has won an impressive trove of accolades including “Master Planned Community of the Year” by The National Association of Home Builders, "LEED for Neighborhood Development Platinum certification" by the U.S. Green Building Council, and “Best-Planned Community in the United States” by Architectural Digest.

Further Comments on MLP's New Directors and CEO

MLP's new management picks appear to be positioning the company to make the most out of its entitled land in Kapalua. I think this is a team that is fully capable of realizing the true value of MLP's land. The company also changed its state of incorporation from Hawaii to Delaware in July of 2022 as it was beginning to assemble this new management team. MLP did this (according to their 2022 10-Q Q3) for "predictability, flexibility, and responsiveness of Delaware law, 2) access to specialized courts, and 3) the enhanced ability to attract and retain qualified candidates for Board of Directors and management."

All of these appointments, in my opinion, indicate that MLP is finally making moves to profit off of the land it has been sitting on for so long. The combination of MLP acquiring an extremely talented management team, the company changing its state of incorporation, and hiring colliers to assess ways to maximize land value has me convinced MLP's share price has a ways to rise before it accurately reflects how valuable the land MLP currently owns really is.

Risks

MLP has seen its average trading volumes sit at 13,539 shares so if you needed to offload a large position you could run into problems finding a buyer or running down the stock price while selling your stock.

Hawaii is also famously hard to get building permits to build just about anything. While I am hoping that the Maui fires put pressure on local governments to expedite some of these processes there is no guarantee this will happen. An inability to get anymore of MLP's agricultural land entitled could significantly hinder the company's ability for future profits beyond the 880 acres in Kapalua. If prospects to be able to entitle more of MLP's land begin to not look very promising, it could greatly impact how much MLP could get for its land or even if there would be anyone or any entity willing to buy that land from MLP.

If MLP begins to somehow run into regulatory hurdles that threaten the future of its building projects at Kapalua I would consider selling. However it is hard to say right now how big those hurdles would have to be and if any large hurdles are likely to show up during the many stages of construction. If the stock reached anywhere close to the $59.11 a share price I would, depending on what management has said about the prospects of future builds, consider selling as this is my value target for MLP's 880 entitled acres at Kapalua.

The aftermath of the Maui fires may change if only temporarily, the way Maui and Hawaii regulate their potential construction projects. Demand for housing after the Maui fires may just be the catalyst that helps MLP and other land owners get approval for future developments on the island.

Conclusion

I believe that MLP is a BUY based solely off of the value of the land it currently plans to develop into housing. A 191.75% upside in the value of this land compared to the company's current share price, from $23.53 to $68.65 per share provides a large enough probability for an adequate return. These extra land holdings that the company owns can therefore be looked at as "extra value" that the company has yet to put to use.

MLP is attempting to lease more of its property. This will enable the company to cover their operating costs associated with running their existing resort and leasing businesses. This income would also help MLP shore up cash to be used in the development of the Kapalua and Hali`imaile properties. I believe that MLP could see an expedited rise in the price of their shares should they begin to generate profits from operations as this would give investors watching this company from the sidelines more confidence that MLP won't slowly sink into a pile of debt as they attempt to shift towards a land development business strategy versus leasing farm fields and running a resort.

If MLP was to able to get more of its land entitled to build on in the future the potential value of MLP would increase astronomically from a 191.75% upside all the way up to the potential of a 580.88% upside compared to the current $23.53 share price. A 580.88% increase would send MLP's share price all the way up north of $160. While there is a lot of uncertainty between MLP's current share price and a $160 share price, the possibility is there, and I believe that this makes MLP a stock to own long-term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zillow's Path To Sustainable Growth Amid Economic Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Amid evolving market conditions, Zillow (NASDAQ:Z) presents a compelling opportunity for long-term investors. Rather than being weighed down by headlines of a weak housing market, Zillow’s strategic shift under new management and its emphasis on innovation in digital real estate have positioned it for growth.

With new management, a potential stabilization of its cash position medium-term, and an early focus towards capitalizing on market gaps particularly prevalent in digital real estate, Zillow will deliver upside long-term if it is able to successfully combat external risks. As part of a two-pronged solution, With a strong focus on expanding its product offerings and maximizing shareholder value delivered from recent acquisitions, Zillow aims to establish a foundation for long-term profitability. Zillow's approach to navigating industry challenges and leveraging its market position could help maintain its competitiveness in the sector. Although there is still much uncertainty about how it will exactly position itself among safe competitors like Rocket Mortgage and Homes dot com, I will give Zillow a hold rating.

Vertical Integration

Zillow has gained market share in response to increasing trends toward convenience in digital real estate. It is supported by the fact buyers are able to book a tour with real estate agents in their area, often as easy as booking a restaurant reservation. With its market valuation of

$13.47B, they have been able to capture a strong competitive lead among digital services that the company does not intend to concentrate upon one of their revenue streams. This aligns with their goal of creating a 'super app' that integrates targeted property advertising and other revenue sources at each step of the home-buying process.

Zillow has shown growth in key performance metrics, reflecting recent success they have had with closer-to-transaction revenue streams such as Zillow Home Loans. Their revenue growth per transaction is up to 80% higher than previously in its first four markets. I project this to be even higher in its next nine enhanced markets scattered across the East, largely because of the Northeast’s 7.7% in existing home prices YOY in August 2024 and the Midwest’s 3.3% increase. Circling back to rising housing markets like Texas or Nevada with large sections of developing real estate, Zillow's cash reserves may allow it to purchase real estate in these markets, potentially creating long-term profit opportunities for its Home Loans segment. Also, its top-line remains on par with housing market developments, yet resistant to low-volume activity directly attributed to a steady increase in 30-year fixed mortgage rates throughout the last year.

That is precisely what Zillow aims to resolve, evolving from a hub of information on real estate in 2006. Here are its core revenue streams:

  • Zillow Premier Agent allows real estate agents on the seller's side to advertise their property to their local area, which varies according to demographics and size of city. Specifically advertised as the “premium experience”

  • Property management companies are charged with listing apartment leases as opposed to residential mortgages

  • Inspectors and other contractors can also participate in their ad revenue model

Zillow has utilized key product features, such as Zillow Home Loans, to enhance its market positioning. By providing free services to customers and collecting fees from sellers, Zillow is attempting to capitalize on growth opportunities in the real estate market, using a model similar to those seen in social networks.

A potential risk Zillow faces is supplying enough buyer’s agents willing to stay on the platform for end-consumers due to the difficulty of communication channels when signing contracts with buyers as part of the NAR agreement. Combined with an inability to discover commission terms with realtors, they spark a chain effect of realtors mutually agreeing to settle in commercial properties or in physical, local markets to avoid implications of the agreement reaching residential transactions.

As a result, when buyers take notice, there might be an overall dissemination of its user base, conflicting with its goals of generating enough cash flows from its “super app” to compete financially with competitors like Redfin (RDFN) and Rocket Mortgage (RKT). They too have been able to capitalize on what analysts at Bankrate observe in recent months, “These lower rates, combined with slowing home price appreciation and wage growth that’s finally outpacing inflation, improve affordability.”

Additionally, if these competitors are positioned in large metropolitan markets like Miami and New Jersey, in the short-term, this may decrease the number of prospective agents in an area willing to tour with customers. However, this will only prove to increase the cash flow cycle of competitor business models as tours are easier to schedule, ultimately moving the home buyer through the process smoothly.

Acquisitions Portfolio

Its portfolio, although currently maximizing service presence with Zillow Group owned Trulia ranking third for real estate websites, lends itself to maximizing customer service experiences. "Zillow's strategic focus on streamlining data collection and providing client access aims to differentiate its approach to real estate CRM by reducing the potential for human error. A notable acquisition under new management is Virtual Staging AI, which supports the company's goal of developing a 'super app' by reducing the need for physical staging.

This aligns with CRM management goals, such as those achieved with Follow Up Boss, by targeting niches in labor-intensive markets. By eliminating daily tasks from agent businesses needed to successfully list properties on Zillow’s Premier Agent, this saves the company from dwindling its general volume of listings every quarter along with labor inefficiencies passed on to Zillow that need to verify listings often multiple times. This strategy aims to reduce costs associated with compensation and maintain favorable profit margins.

In addition, this approach highlights their effort to use technology to enhance the customer experience, enabling a wider understanding of how enhanced markets enabled by Zillow will remain the next differentiating pathway to satisfy eager homebuyers. Subsequently, the company can choose to capitalize on users dispatched to the pipeline of Zillow’s B2C package, becoming a crucial part of the vertical integration strategy. Reducing the stress and anxiety experienced by 60% of home buyers remains a pertinent potential area for improvement.

Financials

Heading into Q2 2024, led by new management Jeremy Wacksman, analysts project a lower price target with limited change in shares outstanding. This alludes to a decrease in EPS, yet they also project a 13.75% increase in total revenue to $2.56B. Residential revenue grew 8% YOY to $409M in Q2 2024. Although rapid increases in home buying for multifamily and single family units remains prevalent in less penetrated cities, Zillow's new approach aims to position it as more than an intermediary in certain markets. Management aims to implement “close to transaction” tactics with its Zillow Home Loans component, supported by their positive operating cash flow position of $135M after June 2024 yet heavy capital expenditures accounting for earnings trending negative short-term after coronavirus disruptions. The company not only purchases properties but also has the option to sell them after making repairs and adjustments.

Although cash-intensive, it has proven to complement well with its growing mortgage business. Acquiring technologically savvy companies, it has transferred customers to its other divisions who are funneled into assistance given by Zillow Home Loans. This approach helps manage risk in markets that may be negatively impacted by reduced realtor activity following the NAR settlement. To support, 18.6% of YOY growth in goodwill demonstrates management confidence to pay a premium for its recent acquisitions. Additionally, the company's cash flow from investing is positive, supported by claimed equity that offsets capital expenditures related to acquisitions and the B2C segment. Zillow’s free cash flow/capex ratio sits at 2.18, while Redfin is closer to 11.35. This indicates ideal range hit by both companies, but capex is being allocated differently because of a difference in revenue streams. Some of these discussed above may lead to potential market capitalization growth for its business going into at least 2-year projections.

Facing nearly $2B of outstanding credit repayments along with settlements of its long-term assets, it should be seen that they have ample leverage because of a large amount of their properties potentially given priority on their platform. Additionally, offsetting some mortgage repayments to subsidiaries or their acquired companies is helpful for decreasing bottom-line income taxation.

Valuation

Because of Zillow’s negative net income over the last five years, projections in cash flows for the next five years in a DCF model would be difficult to forecast. Additionally, the cyclical nature of the company’s enterprise value YOY was a primary factor for choosing metrics ending FY2023. Zillow’s revenues often oscillate, peaking Q4 largely due to shifting market demand for all types of properties. Although this choice of time period, nearly a year old, seems to underestimate their recent performance, many forward-looking valuation metrics like the PE ratio have merely changed from -87.6 in 2023 to -92.9 currently. This provided a substantial basis for me to proceed with both an enterprise value and market capitalization valuation.

Comparing value per share, I discovered net debt accounted for a negligible difference, which prompted me to choose the enterprise valuation to account for changes in net debt that calculates for liquidity of Zillow. Therefore, I chose to proceed with the enterprise valuation.

Aligned with Zillow’s sudden growth in Q4, their current stock price is closer to their 52-week-high. Although I will look for a future opportunity to purchase the stock during a different opportunity, monitoring the stock over the next year for key changes to their “super app” or acquisitions will be important to reverting to this analysis.

The Bottom Line

Zillow's position in the evolving digital real estate marketplaces it in a favorable spot for long-term growth, despite current economic uncertainties. By expanding its B2C offerings and focusing on acquisitions like Zillow Home Loans, the company is strategically positioning itself to capture opportunities in high-traffic markets while managing the cyclical nature of the housing industry.

While potential risks, such as competition from non-cyclical businesses or economic implications of regulation, remain, Zillow’s continued innovation and push toward vertical integration support its vision for future profitability. With a diversified revenue model and commitment to technological advancements, Zillow is poised to navigate industry challenges and maintain its leadership.
